The Zendata gap: IAM provisioned the financial-rag-assistant as a low-risk RAG application (IAM 20). Behavioral telemetry caught a takeover attempt: an insider used hijacked machine credentials to pivot into the agent — raising ZD to 95 while drift score stayed STABLE.

Attack chain: Alex Chen (insider) → Robot API (hijacked) → financial-rag-assistant (compromised).
IdentityTypeDepartmentRole IAM RiskZD RiskGapEventsPIIClassification
Note: Alex Chen appears under two session IDs (agt_285dc16b... and usr_analyst_01). Zendata identity correlation flagged the duplication as part of the active incident. Robot API was the credential pivot used to reach financial-rag-assistant.
Scenario A — API Service Account Usage Profile
Recommendation: ~95% of API Service Account calls are legitimate user-driven traffic. Zendata isolated a 5% slice tied to Alex Chen sessions exhibiting templated submission velocity — rotate the credential and tighten the legitimate caller allowlist.
Attack-attributable traffic: ~5% of 342 calls (~17 events)
Scenario B — financial-rag-assistant Tool Surface (Agent, 365d)
Recommendation: Tool surface itself is narrow and well-scoped. The risk is not over-privilege — it is the upstream caller. Quarantine the agent and revoke credentials of any caller correlated with the takeover signal.
Scenario C — financial-rag-assistant Tool Scope & Takeover Alert
Tool Access by Phase
Risk Score Progression (Monthly)
Drift score stayed STABLE at 0.0000 for the entire window. TAKEOVER_ALERT raised on 2026-04-07 from behavioral signal correlation: 12 critical event records, all attributable to a single incident chain originating from Alex Chen.

Machine / Service Agent Inventory
WHY MACHINE IDENTITIES MATTER FOR IAM
IAM traditionally governs human identity provisioning and lifecycle. Machine identities — service accounts, API keys, AI agents — are provisioned once and rarely reviewed. While IAM assigns low static risk, Zendata behavioral analytics tracks their actual telemetry over time to identify scope drift, PII mishandling, and credential hijack attempts.

The feedback loop: Zendata signals trigger IAM access certifications dynamically based on behavioral anomalies — even when classical drift monitoring shows STABLE.
